pattern: Reachability and rank both fall out of a single BFS from the start cell: distance is the BFS layer, and everything else is just a stable four-way tie-break stacked on top of it.
hints: Treat this as a graph reachability question first: only cells connected to the start through in-bounds, non-wall neighbors ever matter, and every move costs exactly one step.

A breadth-first search from the start visits cells in order of increasing shortest distance, which is exactly the primary ranking key -- collect a cell as a candidate the instant you visit it, if its value falls inside the price window.
Once every candidate has a (distance, price, row, col) tuple, sorting ascending by that tuple gives the ranked list directly; keep the first k entries (or fewer, if that is all that qualifies).

edge_inputs.description: several candidates tie on distance; the tie is broken by price, and there are fewer qualifying cells than k
an in-range item sits behind a wall and is never reachable, so it must not appear in the result
k equals the total cell count and every cell qualifies, exercising the full ranked order
four candidates tie on both distance and price; the order must fall back to row then column
minimum possible grid: a single cell that is also the start
the start cell's own value is a passable non-item cell (value 1), so it must be excluded even though it is technically reachable at distance zero
single row with no walls, k equal to the row length, distances increase linearly with no ties

stress.description: random grid with walls and prices, start cell forced passable, price window and k chosen to exercise most of the ranking logic
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = max(2, min(size, 25))
    n = max(2, min(size, 25))
    grid = [[rng.randint(0, 20) for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(m)]
    sr, sc = rng.randrange(m), rng.randrange(n)
    if grid[sr][sc] == 0:
        grid[sr][sc] = 1
    low = rng.randint(2, 12)
    high = low + rng.randint(0, 10)
    k = rng.randint(1, m * n)
    return [grid, [low, high], [sr, sc], k]

solutions.python:
from collections import deque

class Solution:
    def highestRankedKItems(self, grid, pricing, start, k):
        low, high = pricing[0], pricing[1]
        m, n = len(grid), len(grid[0])
        sr, sc = start[0], start[1]
        visited = [[False] * n for _ in range(m)]
        visited[sr][sc] = True
        q = deque([(sr, sc, 0)])
        items = []
        while q:
            r, c, d = q.popleft()
            price = grid[r][c]
            if low <= price <= high:
                items.append((d, price, r, c))
            for dr, dc in ((1, 0), (-1, 0), (0, 1), (0, -1)):
                nr, nc = r + dr, c + dc
                if 0 <= nr < m and 0 <= nc < n and not visited[nr][nc] and grid[nr][nc] != 0:
                    visited[nr][nc] = True
                    q.append((nr, nc, d + 1))
        items.sort(key=lambda t: (t[0], t[1], t[2], t[3]))
        return [[r, c] for _, _, r, c in items[:k]]
